name: Classroom NGSS Scoring Supports Synthetic Augmentation but Calls for Additional Validity Work
statement: Djagba et al. support synthetic responses as classroom automated-scoring training augmentation for class imbalance and feedback-oriented learning-progression scoring, while explicitly leaving grade-bearing, cross-domain, and broader validity claims open pending generalization studies and expert quality checks.
population_or_context: High school NGSS scientific explanations with 11 binary-coded analytic categories.
time_horizon: Full text retrieved and extracted on 2026-06-03.
quantitative_note: GPT augmentation improved Category 5 F1 from 58.82 to 86.80 and Category 6 F1 from 0.00 to 91.20 in reported held-out evaluations; EASE and ALP were often stronger than GPT augmentation.
caveats: Preprint/conference evidence rather than an operational assessment policy.
The study is framed around classroom learning progression scoring and feedback, not high-stakes summative decisions.
The authors state that synthetic data use requires additional validity work and expert quality checks.
evaluation_method: Fixed 80/20 train-test split with accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 on held-out real student responses.
reported_metrics: Accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 across rubric categories and augmentation strategies.
limitations: Does not establish acceptable use for classroom grades, placement, accountability, or live high-stakes scoring.
Model naming and generated examples should be checked because the paper describes GPT-4 generation but contains one apparent GPT-5 example label.

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase_note: The finding paraphrases the GPT augmentation setup, 80/20 evaluation, F1 improvements, and validity-work limitation.
